heading: Create a Knowledge Base
body:
In this section, you create a [knowledge base](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html)
to load custom data into your vector store.

- Log in to the [AWS Console](https://aws.amazon.com/console/).

- In the upper-left corner, click the Services dropdown menu.

- Click Machine Learning, and then select Amazon Bedrock.

Amazon Bedrock doesn't grant access to FMs (foundation models)
automatically. If you haven't already,
follow the steps to [add model access](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-access.html)
for the **Titan Embeddings G1 - Text** and **Anthropic Claude V2.1** models.

- In the left navigation of the Amazon Bedrock console,
  click Knowledge Bases.

- Click Create and then select Knowledge base with vector store.

- Specify `mongodb-atlas-knowledge-base` as the Knowledge Base name.

- Click Next.

By default, Amazon Bedrock creates a new IAM (Identity and Access Management) role to access
the knowledge base.

- Specify a name for the data source used by the knowledge base.

- Enter the URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) for the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket that contains your data source.
  Or, click Browse S3 and find the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket
  that contains your data source from the list.

- Click Next.

  Amazon Bedrock displays available embedding models that you can use to
  convert your data source's text data into vector embeddings.

- Select the Titan Embeddings G1 - Text model.

* In the Vector database section, select Use an existing vector store.

* Select MongoDB Atlas and configure the following options:

  ![Screenshot of the Amazon Bedrock vector store configuration section.](/_includes/images/amazon-bedrock-vector-store-config.png)

  - For the Hostname, enter the URL (Uniform Resource Locator) for your Atlas cluster
    located in its [connection string](manual/reference/connection-string).
    The hostname uses the following format:

    ```
    <clusterName>.mongodb.net
    ```

  - For the Database name, enter `bedrock_db`.

  - For the Collection name, enter `test`.

  - For the Credentials secret ARN, enter the ARN (Amazon Resource Name)
    for the secret that contains your Atlas cluster credentials.
    To learn more, see [AWS Secrets Manager concepts](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/getting-started.html).

* In the Metadata field mapping section, configure the
  following options to determine the MongoDB Vector Search index and field names
  that Atlas uses to embed and store your data source:

  ![Screenshot of the vector store field mapping configuration section.](/_includes/images/amazon-bedrock-field-mapping-config.png)

  - For the Vector search index name, enter `vector_index`.

  - For the Vector embedding field path, enter `embedding`.

  - For the Text field path, enter `bedrock_text_chunk`.

  - For the Metadata field path, enter `bedrock_metadata`.

  > **Note:** Optionally, you can specify the Text search index name field
  > to configure hybrid search. To learn more, see [Hybrid Search with Amazon Bedrock and Atlas](bedrock-hybrid-search).

* If you [configured an endpoint service](bedrock-configure-privatelink),
  enter your PrivateLink Service Name.

* Click Next.

After reviewing the details for your knowledge base,
click Create knowledge base to finalize your creation.

After Amazon Bedrock creates the knowledge base, it prompts you
to sync your data. In the Data source section,
select your data source and click Sync to sync
the data from the S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket and load it into Atlas.

When the sync completes, if you're using Atlas, you can verify your vector embeddings
by navigating to the `bedrock_db.test` namespace
[in the Atlas UI](atlas-ui-view-collections).
